rawText:
I'm going to talk about a failure of intuition that many of us suffer from. It's really a failure to detect a certain kind of danger. I'm going to describe a scenario that I think is both terrifying and likely to occur, and that's not a good combination, as it turns out. And yet rather than be scared, most of you will feel that what I'm talking about is kind of cool.

I'm going to describe how the gains we make in artificial intelligence could ultimately destroy us. And in fact, I think it's very difficult to see how they won't destroy us or inspire us to destroy ourselves. And yet if you're anything like me, you'll find that it's fun to think about these things. And that response is part of the problem. That response should worry you.

And if I were to convince you in this talk that we were likely to suffer a global famine, either because of climate change or some other catastrophe, and that your grandchildren, or their grandchildren, are very likely to live like this, you wouldn't think, "Interesting. I like this TED Talk." Famine isn't fun. Death by science fiction, on the other hand, is fun, and one of the things that worries me most about the development of AI at this point is that we seem unable to marshal an appropriate emotional response to the dangers that lie ahead. I am unable to marshal this response, and I'm giving this talk.

It's as though we stand before two doors. Behind door number one, we stop making progress in building intelligent machines. Our computer hardware and software just stops getting better for some reason. Now take a moment to consider why this might happen. I mean, given how valuable intelligence and automation are, we will continue to improve our technology if we are at all able to. What could stop us from doing this? A full-scale nuclear war? A global pandemic? An asteroid impact? Justin Bieber becoming president of the United States?

The point is, something would have to destroy civilization as we know it. You have to imagine how bad it would have to be to prevent us from making improvements in our technology permanently, generation after generation. Almost by definition, this is the worst thing that's ever happened in human history.

So the only alternative, and this is what lies behind door number two, is that we continue to improve our intelligent machines year after year after year. At a certain point, we will build machines that are smarter than we are, and once we have machines that are smarter than we are, they will begin to improve themselves. And then we risk what the mathematician IJ Good called an "intelligence explosion," that the process could get away from us.

overview.title: Can We Build AI Without Losing Control Over It?

sentences.original: I'm going to talk about a failure of intuition that many of us suffer from.
sentences.translation: 저는 우리 대부분이 겪고 있는 직관의 실패에 대해 이야기하려고 합니다.

grammarElements.text: that many of us suffer from
grammarElements.label: 관계대명사절 (that — failure를 수식)

vocabulary.example: She had an intuition that something was wrong.
Many people suffer from anxiety.
The failure of the system caused major problems.

expressions.usage: 강연 시작 시 주제를 소개하는 전형적인 오프닝
추상적 개념의 결함을 명사구로 표현할 때

patterns.pattern: I'm going to talk about [noun phrase] that [clause]
patterns.explanation: TED 강연의 전형적인 오프닝 패턴. 주제를 소개하면서 관계절로 부연 설명을 추가.
examples: I'm going to talk about a problem that affects millions of people.
I'm going to talk about a discovery that could change everything.

drillQuestions.question: I'm going to talk _____ a failure of intuition.
A failure of intuition that many of us suffer _____.

sentences.notes: "suffer from"은 관계대명사절 안에서 전치사가 뒤에 남아 있는(stranded preposition) 형태. "a failure of intuition that many of us suffer from" = "a failure of intuition from which many of us suffer". 구어체에서는 전치사를 뒤에 두는 것이 자연스럽다.

koreanAlignment.en: that many of us suffer from

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 문제 제기(problem statement) — 청중의 호기심을 유발하면서 강연 주제를 제시하는 오프닝

sentences.original: It's really a failure to detect a certain kind of danger.
sentences.translation: 이것은 정말로 특정 종류의 위험을 감지하지 못하는 실패입니다.

grammarElements.text: a failure to detect a certain kind of danger
grammarElements.label: 보어 (명사 + to부정사)

vocabulary.example: The sensor can detect changes in temperature.
There are certain rules you must follow.

expressions.usage: 동작의 실패를 명사화하여 표현할 때
구체적으로 지정하지 않으면서 특정 유형을 가리킬 때

patterns.pattern: It's really a failure to [verb] [object]
patterns.explanation: 앞서 언급한 것을 구체화하며 재정의하는 패턴. "failure to + 동사"로 행위의 결여를 표현.
examples: It's really a failure to communicate clearly.
It's really a failure to understand the risks.

drillQuestions.question: It's really a failure _____ detect a certain kind of danger.
A _____ kind of danger that we cannot detect.

sentences.notes: "a failure to detect"는 "failure to + 동사원형" 구조로, 해야 할 행동을 하지 못하는 것을 의미. "a failure of intuition"(직관의 실패)를 "a failure to detect danger"(위험 감지 실패)로 구체화하고 있다.

sentences.original: I'm going to describe a scenario that I think is both terrifying and likely to occur, and that's not a good combination, as it turns out.
sentences.translation: 저는 무섭기도 하고 일어날 가능성도 높은 시나리오를 설명하려고 하는데, 결국 이것은 좋은 조합이 아닙니다.

grammarElements.text: that I think is both terrifying and likely to occur
grammarElements.label: 관계대명사절 (삽입절 "I think" 포함)

grammarElements.text: and that's not a good combination
grammarElements.label: 등위절

vocabulary.example: Let me describe a worst-case scenario.
The horror movie was absolutely terrifying.
The combination of heat and humidity was unbearable.

expressions.usage: 두 가지 특성을 동시에 가짐을 표현
예상과 다르거나 결과를 드러낼 때 사용하는 삽입구

patterns.pattern: [Subject] that I think is both [A] and [B]
patterns.explanation: 관계절 안에 "I think"를 삽입하여 주관적 판단임을 나타내는 패턴. 학술적 글에서도 자주 사용.
examples: A problem that I think is both urgent and solvable.
A decision that I think is both brave and necessary.

drillQuestions.question: A scenario that I think is _____ terrifying and likely to occur.
That's not a good combination, _____ it turns out.

sentences.notes: "that I think is"에서 "I think"는 삽입절(parenthetical)로, 빼도 문법적으로 완전한 문장이 된다: "a scenario that is both terrifying and likely to occur". 이런 삽입은 주관적 의견임을 드러내면서도 부드럽게 표현하는 효과가 있다.

koreanAlignment.en: that I think is both terrifying

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 역설적 유머(paradoxical humor) — 무섭고 가능성 높은 것이 "좋지 않은 조합"이라는 절제된 표현으로 웃음 유발

sentences.original: And yet rather than be scared, most of you will feel that what I'm talking about is kind of cool.
sentences.translation: 그런데도 무서워하기보다는, 여러분 대부분은 제가 말하는 것이 꽤 멋지다고 느낄 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: that what I'm talking about is kind of cool
grammarElements.label: that절 (feel의 목적절)

vocabulary.example: I chose to walk rather than drive.
The children were scared of the dark.
It's kind of interesting, don't you think?

expressions.usage: 앞의 내용과 모순되는 사실을 소개할 때
구어체에서 완곡하게 긍정적 평가를 할 때

patterns.pattern: Rather than [verb], [subject] will [verb] ...
patterns.explanation: 예상되는 반응 대신 실제 반응을 대비하는 패턴. rather than 뒤에 동사원형이 온다.
examples: Rather than feel concerned, people tend to dismiss the warning.
Rather than take action, most companies wait and see.

drillQuestions.question: Rather _____ be scared, most of you will feel it is cool.
What I'm talking about is _____ of cool.

sentences.notes: "rather than be scared"에서 rather than 뒤에 동사원형(bare infinitive)이 온다. "rather than being scared"도 가능하지만, 동사원형이 더 격식 있는 표현. "kind of"는 구어에서 "kinda"로 줄여 발음되기도 한다.

sentences.original: I'm going to describe how the gains we make in artificial intelligence could ultimately destroy us.
sentences.translation: 저는 인공지능에서의 우리의 성과가 어떻게 궁극적으로 우리를 파괴할 수 있는지 설명하려고 합니다.

grammarElements.text: how the gains we make in artificial intelligence could ultimately destroy us
grammarElements.label: 명사절 (how — describe의 목적어)

vocabulary.example: The company made significant gains in market share.
Artificial intelligence is transforming every industry.
The decision will ultimately affect everyone.
The fire destroyed the entire building.

expressions.usage: 특정 분야에서의 진보를 표현할 때
장기적인 위험성을 경고할 때

patterns.pattern: how [noun] we make in [field] could [verb] us
patterns.explanation: 특정 분야의 진보가 미칠 수 있는 영향을 설명하는 패턴. how절이 목적어 역할.
examples: How the progress we make in genetics could transform medicine.
How the choices we make in policy could shape our future.

drillQuestions.question: The gains we make in artificial intelligence could _____ destroy us.
I'm going to describe _____ AI could destroy us.

sentences.notes: "the gains we make"에서 관계대명사 that/which가 생략되어 있다. 원래는 "the gains that we make". 이 문장은 강연의 핵심 주장을 단도직입적으로 제시한다. "could"를 사용하여 가능성을 나타내면서도, "destroy us"라는 극단적 표현으로 긴장감을 조성.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 충격적 주장(shock thesis) — 강연 초반에 "AI가 우리를 파괴할 수 있다"는 강렬한 주장으로 주의를 집중

sentences.original: And in fact, I think it's very difficult to see how they won't destroy us or inspire us to destroy ourselves.
sentences.translation: 사실, 저는 그것들이 우리를 파괴하지 않거나 우리 스스로를 파괴하도록 유발하지 않을 방법을 찾기가 매우 어렵다고 생각합니다.

grammarElements.text: or inspire us to destroy ourselves
grammarElements.label: 등위절 (or)

vocabulary.example: The speech inspired people to take action.
In fact, the situation is much worse than expected.

expressions.phrase: it's very difficult to see how
expressions.usage: 어떤 결과를 피하기 어렵다는 것을 간접적으로 표현
간접적인 원인 관계를 표현할 때

patterns.pattern: It's very difficult to see how [subject] won't [verb]
patterns.explanation: 이중 부정을 통해 "거의 확실히 ~할 것"이라는 의미를 간접적으로 표현하는 패턴.
examples: It's very difficult to see how this won't end badly.
It's very difficult to see how prices won't increase.

drillQuestions.question: It's very difficult to see _____ they won't destroy us.
They could inspire us _____ destroy ourselves.

sentences.notes: "it's difficult to see how they won't"는 이중 부정의 효과로, "거의 확실히 파괴할 것이다"라는 강한 주장을 우회적으로 표현. 직접 "they will destroy us"라고 하는 것보다 학술적이고 사려 깊은 인상을 준다.

koreanAlignment.en: I think it's very difficult to see
or inspire us to destroy ourselves
koreanAlignment.ko: 또는 우리 스스로를 파괴하도록 유발하지 않을

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 이중 부정(litotes) — "파괴하지 않을 방법을 보기 어렵다"는 우회적 표현으로 주장을 강화

sentences.original: And yet if you're anything like me, you'll find that it's fun to think about these things.
sentences.translation: 그런데 여러분이 저와 조금이라도 비슷하다면, 이런 것들에 대해 생각하는 것이 재미있다는 것을 알게 될 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: that it's fun to think about these things
grammarElements.label: that절 (find의 목적절)

vocabulary.example: If it's anything like last year, it'll be great.

expressions.usage: 청중과 공감대를 형성하며 자신의 경험을 일반화할 때
지적 호기심을 표현할 때

patterns.pattern: If you're anything like me, you'll find that ...
patterns.explanation: 화자와 청중 사이의 공감대를 형성하는 패턴. 자신의 경험을 청중에게 투영.
examples: If you're anything like me, you'll find that mornings are the hardest.
If you're anything like me, you'll agree this is fascinating.

drillQuestions.question: If you're _____ like me, you'll find it fun.
You'll _____ that it's fun to think about these things.

sentences.notes: "anything like me"에서 anything은 "조금이라도, 어떤 면에서든"이라는 의미로, "if you are at all similar to me"와 같은 뜻. 이 표현은 청중을 자연스럽게 화자의 관점으로 끌어들이는 수사적 기법.

koreanAlignment.en: it's fun to think about these things
koreanAlignment.ko: 이런 것들에 대해 생각하는 것이 재미있다는 것

sentences.original: And that response is part of the problem. That response should worry you.
sentences.translation: 그리고 그런 반응이 문제의 일부입니다. 그 반응이 여러분을 걱정하게 만들어야 합니다.

vocabulary.example: The public response was overwhelmingly positive.
The test results should worry everyone.

expressions.usage: 어떤 것이 문제를 구성하는 요소임을 지적할 때
경각심을 촉구하는 강한 표현

patterns.pattern: [X] is part of the problem. [X] should worry you.
patterns.explanation: 같은 주어를 반복하며 문제점을 지적하고 경각심을 촉구하는 강렬한 2문장 패턴.
examples: Our complacency is part of the problem. Our complacency should worry you.
This attitude is part of the problem. This attitude should worry you.

drillQuestions.question: That response is _____ of the problem.
That response _____ worry you.

sentences.notes: 두 문장 모두 "That response"로 시작하는 의도적 반복(anaphora). 첫 문장에서 문제를 정의하고, 두 번째에서 청중에게 직접 경고하는 구조. 간결하고 강렬한 메시지 전달 기법.

koreanAlignment.ko: 그 반응이 여러분을 걱정하게 만들어야 합니다

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 수미상관 반복(anaphora) — "That response"를 반복하여 메시지를 각인시킴

sentences.original: And if I were to convince you in this talk that we were likely to suffer a global famine, either because of climate change or some other catastrophe, and that your grandchildren, or their grandchildren, are very likely to live like this, you wouldn't think, "Interesting. I like this TED Talk."
sentences.translation: 만약 제가 이 강연에서 기후변화나 다른 재앙 때문에 우리가 세계적 기근을 겪을 가능성이 높다고, 그리고 여러분의 손자녀나 그 손자녀들이 이렇게 살게 될 가능성이 매우 높다고 설득한다면, 여러분은 "흥미롭네요. 이 TED 강연 좋아요"라고 생각하지 않을 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: if I were to convince you
grammarElements.label: 조건절 (가정법 — were to)

grammarElements.text: that we were likely to suffer a global famine
grammarElements.label: that절 (convince의 내용)

grammarElements.text: either because of climate change or some other catastrophe
grammarElements.label: 부사구 (이유 — either A or B)

grammarElements.text: and that your grandchildren ... are very likely to live like this
grammarElements.label: 두 번째 that절 (convince의 내용)

vocabulary.example: The drought led to a terrible famine.
A nuclear war would be a catastrophe for humanity.
She convinced me to change my mind.
She has five grandchildren.

expressions.usage: 비현실적이거나 가상의 상황을 가정할 때 사용하는 격식체
두 가지 가능한 원인이나 선택지를 제시할 때

patterns.pattern: If I were to [verb] you that ..., you wouldn't [verb]
patterns.explanation: 가정법 과거("were to")를 사용하여 가상의 시나리오와 그 반응을 대비하는 고급 패턴.
examples: If I were to tell you that the economy is collapsing, you wouldn't laugh.
If I were to show you the data, you wouldn't ignore it.

drillQuestions.question: If I _____ to convince you of a global famine, you wouldn't laugh.
We were likely to suffer a global _____.

sentences.notes: 이 문장은 강연에서 가장 긴 문장 중 하나로, "if I were to" 가정법으로 시작하여 두 개의 that절을 거쳐 주절 "you wouldn't think"에 도달한다. "were to"는 be to 구문의 가정법 형태로, 격식 있는 가정을 표현. 청중의 이중잣대(AI 위험은 재미있지만 기근은 재미없다)를 지적하는 핵심 논거.

koreanAlignment.en: if I were to convince you
that we were likely to suffer a global famine
either because of climate change or some other catastrophe
your grandchildren are very likely to live like this
koreanAlignment.ko: 손자녀들이 이렇게 살게 될 가능성이 높다고

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 비유적 대비(analogical contrast) — AI 위험과 기근을 대비하여 청중의 부적절한 감정 반응을 폭로

sentences.original: Famine isn't fun. Death by science fiction, on the other hand, is fun, and one of the things that worries me most about the development of AI at this point is that we seem unable to marshal an appropriate emotional response to the dangers that lie ahead.
sentences.translation: 기근은 재미없습니다. 반면에 공상과학에 의한 죽음은 재미있습니다. 그리고 이 시점에서 AI 발전에 대해 저를 가장 걱정하게 하는 것 중 하나는 우리가 앞에 놓인 위험에 적절한 감정적 반응을 동원할 수 없는 것 같다는 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: Death by science fiction, on the other hand, is fun
grammarElements.label: 주절 (대조의 두 번째 부분)

grammarElements.text: one of the things that worries me most about the development of AI
grammarElements.label: 주어 (관계대명사절 포함)

grammarElements.text: that we seem unable to marshal an appropriate emotional response
grammarElements.label: that절 (보어)

grammarElements.text: to the dangers that lie ahead
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (관계대명사절 포함)

vocabulary.example: The government tried to marshal support for the new policy.
Please wear appropriate clothing for the event.
We don't know what challenges lie ahead.

expressions.usage: 대조되는 두 가지를 비교할 때

expressions.phrase: one of the things that worries me most
expressions.meaning: 저를 가장 걱정하게 하는 것 중 하나
expressions.usage: 여러 우려 중 핵심적인 것을 꺼낼 때
AI로 인한 인류 멸망을 비유적으로 표현

patterns.pattern: One of the things that worries me most about [X] is that [clause]
patterns.explanation: 핵심 우려를 제시하는 격식 있는 패턴. "one of the things that"로 시작하여 중요도를 강조.
examples: One of the things that worries me most about education is that students lack critical thinking.
One of the things that worries me most about healthcare is that access is unequal.

drillQuestions.question: We seem unable to _____ an appropriate emotional response.
The dangers that _____ ahead are real.

sentences.notes: "marshal"은 군사적 어원의 동사로, "감정적 반응을 동원하다"라는 비유적 의미로 사용. 이 단어 선택 자체가 위협의 심각성을 암시. "Death by science fiction"은 Sam Harris가 만든 인상적인 표현으로, AI 위험이 SF처럼 느껴져서 심각하게 받아들이지 못하는 현상을 포착.

koreanAlignment.en: Death by science fiction, on the other hand, is fun
one of the things that worries me most
koreanAlignment.ko: 저를 가장 걱정하게 하는 것 중 하나는

koreanAlignment.en: about the development of AI at this point
is that we seem unable to marshal
koreanAlignment.ko: 동원할 수 없는 것 같다는 것입니다

koreanAlignment.en: to the dangers that lie ahead

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 자기비판(self-implication) — "I am unable to marshal this response, and I'm giving this talk"로 이어지며 자신도 포함시키는 정직한 수사

sentences.original: I am unable to marshal this response, and I'm giving this talk.
sentences.translation: 저도 이 반응을 동원할 수 없습니다. 그런데 이 강연을 하고 있는 사람이 바로 저입니다.

vocabulary.example: He was unable to attend the meeting.

expressions.phrase: I am unable to ... and I'm giving this talk
expressions.meaning: 저도 ~할 수 없는데 이 강연을 하고 있습니다
expressions.usage: 자기 모순을 인정하면서 문제의 심각성을 강조

patterns.pattern: I am unable to [verb] ..., and I'm [doing something relevant]
patterns.explanation: 자신도 문제에 해당됨을 고백하는 자기비판 패턴. 청중과의 신뢰를 구축.
examples: I am unable to solve this problem, and I'm the expert.
I am unable to follow my own advice, and I'm a doctor.

drillQuestions.question: I am _____ to marshal this response.

sentences.notes: 이 짧은 문장은 앞의 긴 문장을 강화하는 핵심 펀치라인. "나조차도 적절한 감정 반응을 못하는데, 바로 그 문제에 대해 강연하고 있다"는 역설이 유머와 동시에 문제의 심각성을 전달한다.

koreanAlignment.en: I am unable to marshal this response
koreanAlignment.ko: 저도 이 반응을 동원할 수 없습니다

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 자기 역설(self-paradox) — 자신의 모순을 인정함으로써 문제의 보편성과 심각성을 동시에 전달

sentences.original: It's as though we stand before two doors.
sentences.translation: 마치 우리가 두 개의 문 앞에 서 있는 것과 같습니다.

grammarElements.text: as though we stand before two doors
grammarElements.label: 양태절 (as though — 마치 ~인 것처럼)

vocabulary.example: She looked as though she had seen a ghost.
We stand before a great opportunity.

expressions.usage: 비유적 상황을 설정할 때. as if와 동의.
두 가지 선택지 또는 시나리오를 은유적으로 표현

patterns.pattern: It's as though we [verb] ...
patterns.explanation: 비유적 상황을 설정하여 복잡한 개념을 시각적으로 전달하는 패턴.
examples: It's as though we live in two different worlds.
It's as though we have no choice at all.

drillQuestions.question: It's as _____ we stand before two doors.
We stand _____ two doors.

sentences.notes: "as though"는 "as if"와 동의어로, 비유적 상황을 제시. 여기서 "two doors"는 인류의 미래에 대한 두 가지 시나리오를 상징하는 은유. 이 문장부터 강연의 논리적 프레임워크가 시작된다.

koreanAlignment.ko: 우리가 두 개의 문 앞에 서 있는

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 은유(metaphor) — "두 개의 문"이라는 시각적 은유로 복잡한 논리 구조를 직관적으로 전달

sentences.original: Behind door number one, we stop making progress in building intelligent machines.
sentences.translation: 첫 번째 문 뒤에서는, 우리가 지능적인 기계를 만드는 데 있어 진보를 멈춥니다.

vocabulary.example: We have made great progress in medicine.
Dolphins are highly intelligent animals.

expressions.usage: 발전이 중단되는 상황을 표현
선택지나 시나리오를 게임쇼 형식으로 제시

patterns.pattern: Behind door number [X], [scenario]
patterns.explanation: TV 게임쇼에서 유래한 선택지 제시 패턴. 시나리오를 흥미롭게 프레이밍.
examples: Behind door number one, we find a booming economy.
Behind door number two, we face a global crisis.

drillQuestions.question: We stop _____ progress in building intelligent machines.
_____ door number one, we stop making progress.

sentences.notes: "stop making"에서 stop + 동명사(making)는 "하던 것을 멈추다"라는 의미. "stop to make"(~하기 위해 멈추다)와 구별해야 한다. "door number one"은 미국 TV 게임쇼(Let's Make a Deal)에서 유래한 문화적 표현.
pronunciationNotes: progress: /ˈprɑːɡres/ (명사) — "프라그레스", 명사일 때 강세는 첫 음절

sentences.original: Our computer hardware and software just stops getting better for some reason.
sentences.translation: 우리의 컴퓨터 하드웨어와 소프트웨어가 어떤 이유로든 더 이상 발전하지 않습니다.

vocabulary.example: The hardware needs to be upgraded.
We need to update the software regularly.

expressions.usage: 발전의 갑작스러운 중단을 단순하게 표현
이유를 특정하지 않고 모호하게 표현

patterns.pattern: [Subject] just stops [verb-ing] for some reason
patterns.explanation: 불명확한 이유로 무언가가 중단되는 상황을 설명하는 구어적 패턴.
examples: The economy just stops growing for some reason.
Innovation just stops happening for some reason.

drillQuestions.question: Our computer hardware just stops _____ better.
It stops for _____ reason.

sentences.notes: "stops getting better"는 stop + 동명사 구조. "just"는 여기서 "단순히, 그냥"이라는 의미로, 갑작스럽고 설명 불가능한 중단을 강조. 이 문장의 단순한 어조는 "이런 시나리오가 얼마나 비현실적인지"를 부각시키기 위한 의도적 표현.

sentences.original: I mean, given how valuable intelligence and automation are, we will continue to improve our technology if we are at all able to.
sentences.translation: 제 말은, 지능과 자동화가 얼마나 가치 있는지를 고려하면, 조금이라도 가능하다면 우리는 계속 기술을 발전시킬 것이라는 뜻입니다.

grammarElements.text: given how valuable intelligence and automation are
grammarElements.label: 분사구문 (given — ~을 고려하면)

grammarElements.text: if we are at all able to
grammarElements.label: 조건절 (if)

vocabulary.example: Automation has transformed the manufacturing industry.
Time is the most valuable resource.
Given the circumstances, we did our best.

expressions.usage: 전제 조건을 설정하며 논리적 귀결을 이끌어 낼 때
최소한의 가능성이라도 있다면이라는 강조

patterns.pattern: Given how [adjective] [X] is, we will [verb] if we are at all able to
patterns.explanation: 가치 판단을 전제로 행동의 필연성을 논증하는 패턴.
examples: Given how important education is, we will invest in it if we are at all able to.
Given how dangerous this threat is, we will respond if we can.

drillQuestions.question: _____ how valuable intelligence is, we will continue.
We will continue if we are _____ all able to.

sentences.notes: "given"은 여기서 "~을 고려하면"이라는 전치사적 용법. "at all"은 "조금이라도"라는 강조로, 가능하기만 하면 반드시 할 것이라는 논리를 완성. 이 문장은 "기술 발전이 멈추는 것은 거의 불가능하다"는 주장의 근거.

koreanAlignment.en: given how valuable intelligence and automation are
koreanAlignment.ko: 지능과 자동화가 얼마나 가치 있는지를 고려하면

koreanAlignment.en: we will continue to improve our technology
if we are at all able to

sentences.original: What could stop us from doing this? A full-scale nuclear war? A global pandemic? An asteroid impact? Justin Bieber becoming president of the United States?
sentences.translation: 무엇이 우리를 막을 수 있을까요? 전면 핵전쟁? 세계적 팬데믹? 소행성 충돌? 저스틴 비버가 미국 대통령이 되는 것?

grammarElements.text: A full-scale nuclear war? A global pandemic? An asteroid impact? Justin Bieber becoming president ...?
grammarElements.label: 수사적 질문 연속 (열거)

vocabulary.example: The conflict escalated into a full-scale war.
The COVID-19 pandemic affected the entire world.
An asteroid impact could wipe out life on Earth.

expressions.usage: stop A from B 구문으로 방해/저지를 표현
최대 규모의 군사적 충돌을 표현

patterns.pattern: What could stop us from [verb-ing]? [A]? [B]? [C]?
patterns.explanation: 수사적 질문 후 극단적 사례를 나열하며 "아무것도 막을 수 없다"는 점을 강조하는 패턴.
examples: What could stop us from communicating? A power outage? A natural disaster?
What could prevent progress? War? Famine? Economic collapse?

drillQuestions.question: What could stop us _____ doing this?
A full-_____ nuclear war could stop progress.

sentences.notes: 수사적 질문(rhetorical questions)을 연속으로 나열하는 강력한 수사법. 핵전쟁, 팬데믹, 소행성 같은 극단적 사례를 나열하다가 마지막에 "저스틴 비버 대통령"이라는 농담을 넣어 긴장을 풀어준다. 2016년 강연 당시의 시대적 유머.

koreanAlignment.en: What could stop us from doing this?
Justin Bieber becoming president of the United States?
koreanAlignment.ko: 저스틴 비버가 미국 대통령이 되는 것?

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 점층법(climax) + 반전 유머(bathos) — 극단적 재앙을 나열한 후 유머러스한 항목으로 긴장을 해소

sentences.original: The point is, something would have to destroy civilization as we know it.
sentences.translation: 요점은, 무언가가 우리가 아는 문명을 파괴해야 한다는 것입니다.

vocabulary.example: Ancient civilizations developed complex societies.
The point is, we need to act now.

expressions.usage: 현재 상태의 무언가가 완전히 변하거나 파괴될 때
가정적 상황에서의 필요성을 표현

patterns.pattern: The point is, [subject] would have to [verb] ...
patterns.explanation: 논의를 요약하며 핵심을 전달하는 패턴. 강연에서 논리를 정리할 때 사용.
examples: The point is, we would have to completely redesign the system.
The point is, someone would have to take responsibility.

drillQuestions.question: Something would have to destroy civilization _____ we know it.
The _____ is, something would have to go wrong.

sentences.notes: "as we know it"는 "우리가 알고 있는 형태의"라는 관용구로, "civilization as we know it"(현재 우리가 아는 문명)처럼 주로 큰 변화나 파괴의 맥락에서 사용. "would have to"는 가정법으로, 첫 번째 문(기술 발전 중단)이 실현되려면 얼마나 극단적인 사건이 필요한지를 강조.

sentences.original: You have to imagine how bad it would have to be to prevent us from making improvements in our technology permanently, generation after generation.
sentences.translation: 영구적으로, 세대를 거듭하면서 우리가 기술을 개선하는 것을 막으려면 얼마나 나빠야 하는지 상상해야 합니다.

grammarElements.text: how bad it would have to be
grammarElements.label: 명사절 (how — imagine의 목적어)

grammarElements.text: to prevent us from making improvements
grammarElements.label: 목적 부사구 (to부정사)

vocabulary.example: The damage was permanently done.
There has been a significant improvement in performance.
Vaccines help prevent diseases.

expressions.usage: 매우 오랜 기간, 지속적으로를 표현하는 반복 구조
prevent A from B 구문

patterns.pattern: You have to imagine how [adjective] it would have to be to [verb]
patterns.explanation: 가정적 상황의 극단성을 상상하도록 촉구하는 패턴. 논리적 귀류법의 일종.
examples: You have to imagine how strong it would have to be to break this.
You have to imagine how rich it would have to be to fund this project alone.

drillQuestions.question: How bad it would have to be to _____ us from making improvements.
Generation _____ generation, we keep improving.

sentences.notes: "would have to be"는 가정법 과거의 필요성 표현으로, "~해야 할 것이다(하지만 현실적으로 그런 일은 없을 것이다)"라는 함의. "generation after generation"은 "[명사] after [명사]" 반복 구조로 지속성을 강조.

koreanAlignment.en: how bad it would have to be
to prevent us from making improvements

sentences.original: Almost by definition, this is the worst thing that's ever happened in human history.
sentences.translation: 거의 정의에 의해서, 이것은 인류 역사상 일어난 최악의 사건입니다.

grammarElements.text: that's ever happened in human history
grammarElements.label: 관계대명사절 (현재완료 + ever)

vocabulary.example: A bachelor, by definition, is unmarried.

expressions.meaning: 거의 정의에 의해서, 논리적으로 거의 필연적으로
expressions.usage: 논리적 추론의 결론이 거의 자명할 때

expressions.phrase: the worst thing that's ever happened
expressions.usage: 최상급 + ever + 현재완료로 역사적 극단을 표현

patterns.pattern: Almost by definition, this is the [superlative] [noun] that's ever [past participle]
patterns.explanation: 논리적 필연성을 강조하며 최상급으로 극단적 결론을 제시하는 학술적 패턴.
examples: Almost by definition, this is the biggest challenge we've ever faced.
Almost by definition, this is the most important decision ever made.

drillQuestions.question: Almost by _____, this is the worst thing in history.
The worst thing that's _____ happened in human history.

sentences.notes: "by definition"은 학술적/논리적 표현으로, "논리적으로 당연히"라는 의미. "almost"를 붙여 절대적 주장을 약간 완화하면서도 논리적 필연성을 유지. 이 문장은 논리적 귀결: "기술 발전이 멈추려면 문명이 파괴되어야 하므로, 그것은 정의상 인류 역사 최악의 사건이 된다."
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 논리적 귀결(logical conclusion) — "정의에 의해"라는 표현으로 결론의 불가피성을 학술적으로 강조

sentences.original: So the only alternative, and this is what lies behind door number two, is that we continue to improve our intelligent machines year after year after year.
sentences.translation: 따라서 유일한 대안은, 그리고 이것이 두 번째 문 뒤에 있는 것인데, 해마다 해마다 우리의 지능적인 기계를 계속 개선하는 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: and this is what lies behind door number two
grammarElements.label: 삽입절 (부연 설명)

grammarElements.text: that we continue to improve our intelligent machines
grammarElements.label: that절 (보어)

vocabulary.example: We need to find an alternative solution.

expressions.usage: 다른 선택지가 없음을 강조할 때
세 번의 반복으로 지속성과 불가피성을 극대화
앞서 설정한 은유를 회수하며 내용을 전개

patterns.pattern: The only alternative is that [clause]
patterns.explanation: 논리적으로 유일한 결론을 제시하는 패턴. 앞의 논증을 마무리.
examples: The only alternative is that we adapt to the changing climate.
The only alternative is that we invest more in education.

drillQuestions.question: The only _____ is that we continue to improve.
We improve our machines year _____ year after year.

sentences.notes: "year after year after year"는 통상 두 번 반복(year after year)하는 것을 세 번으로 확장하여 끝없는 지속을 강조하는 수사법. 삽입절 "and this is what lies behind door number two"는 앞에서 설정한 "두 개의 문" 은유를 회수(callback)하여 논리적 구조를 완성.

koreanAlignment.en: and this is what lies behind door number two
koreanAlignment.ko: 이것이 두 번째 문 뒤에 있는 것인데

koreanAlignment.en: is that we continue to improve

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 은유 회수(callback) + 삼중 반복(tricolon) — "두 번째 문" 은유를 회수하고, "year after year after year"로 불가피성 강조

sentences.original: At a certain point, we will build machines that are smarter than we are, and once we have machines that are smarter than we are, they will begin to improve themselves.
sentences.translation: 어느 시점에서, 우리는 우리보다 더 똑똑한 기계를 만들 것이고, 우리보다 더 똑똑한 기계를 갖게 되면, 그것들은 스스로를 개선하기 시작할 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: machines that are smarter than we are
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (관계대명사절 포함)

grammarElements.text: once we have machines that are smarter than we are
grammarElements.label: 종속절 (once — 일단 ~하면)

grammarElements.text: they will begin to improve themselves
grammarElements.label: 주절

vocabulary.example: At a certain point, you have to make a decision.
She is smarter than most of her classmates.

expressions.usage: 특정 조건이 충족된 후의 결과를 설명할 때
자기 개선 능력의 시작을 표현

patterns.pattern: Once we have [X], [X] will begin to [verb]
patterns.explanation: 특정 임계점을 넘으면 자동으로 진행되는 과정을 설명하는 패턴.
examples: Once we have autonomous vehicles, they will begin to reshape cities.
Once we have the data, we will begin to see patterns.

drillQuestions.question: _____ we have machines that are smarter, they will improve themselves.
Machines that are smarter _____ we are.

sentences.notes: "smarter than we are"에서 "we are"를 생략하지 않고 사용하여 격식체를 유지. 구어에서는 "smarter than us"가 자연스럽지만, 학술적 맥락에서는 "smarter than we are"가 문법적으로 정확. "machines that are smarter than we are"의 반복이 논리적 연쇄(도미노 효과)를 강화.

koreanAlignment.en: that are smarter than we are
that are smarter than we are
they will begin to improve themselves

sentences.original: And then we risk what the mathematician IJ Good called an "intelligence explosion," that the process could get away from us.
sentences.translation: 그러면 우리는 수학자 IJ 굿이 "지능 폭발"이라고 불렀던 것, 즉 그 과정이 우리의 통제를 벗어날 수 있다는 위험에 처하게 됩니다.

grammarElements.text: what the mathematician IJ Good called an "intelligence explosion"
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (what 명사절)

grammarElements.text: that the process could get away from us
grammarElements.label: 동격절 (intelligence explosion을 설명)

vocabulary.example: We risk losing everything if we delay.
An intelligence explosion could happen once AI surpasses human intelligence.
The situation got away from the authorities.
The mathematician proved a famous theorem.

expressions.usage: 다른 사람의 개념이나 용어를 인용할 때
상황이나 과정이 통제 불능 상태가 될 때

patterns.pattern: We risk what [authority] called [concept], that [explanation]
patterns.explanation: 권위자의 개념을 인용하고 동격절로 설명을 추가하는 학술적 패턴.
examples: We risk what economists call a "debt trap," that the economy cannot recover.
We face what scientists call a "tipping point," that the damage becomes irreversible.

drillQuestions.question: We risk what the mathematician IJ Good _____ an "intelligence explosion."
The process could get _____ from us.

sentences.notes: IJ Good(1916-2009)은 영국 수학자로, 1965년 "intelligence explosion" 개념을 제안. "get away from us"는 일상적 구동사를 사용하여 복잡한 개념(통제 불능의 초지능)을 청중이 직관적으로 이해할 수 있게 만든다. 동격절 "that the process could get away from us"는 "intelligence explosion"이 무엇인지를 풀어서 설명.

koreanAlignment.en: what the mathematician IJ Good called
that the process could get away from us
koreanAlignment.ko: 즉 그 과정이 우리의 통제를 벗어날 수 있다는

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 권위 인용(appeal to authority) — 수학자 IJ Good의 개념을 인용하여 주장에 학문적 신뢰성 부여
